coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots grafted by oil-soluble polymer brushes derived from bis-alkyl chain monomers as a lubricant additive of poly-α-olefin with outstanding friction-reducing, anti-wear and anti-rust properties
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Siwei
Chen, Qingqing
Wang, Baogang
Lu, Hongsheng
coredata.dc:description: The application of carbon dots (CDs) as lubricant additives of nonpolar oils such as poly-α-olefins (PAOs) has been greatly hindered because most of CDs derived from the bottom-up method were water-soluble. In this work, the oil-soluble polymer brushes grafted CDs were synthesized by the surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization of bis-alkyl chain monomers. As expected, the polymer grafted CDs exhibited the excellent long-term dispersion stability in PAO owing to the principle of similar compatibility. As the lubricant additives of PAO4, the CDs showed the best friction-reducing and anti-wear properties when the polymerization time was 2 h. Adding 1 wt% of CDs could make the coefficient of friction and wear volume of PAO4 reduce by 64.1 % and 49.6 %, respectively. Moreover, both the tribological properties and anti-rust performance of CDs were superior to the commercially used ZDDP. The distinguished friction-reducing and anti-wear properties of CDs were attributed to the synergistic lubrication of carbon cores and polymer brushes. The rolling, repairing and polishing action of carbon cores, together with the tribo-reactions of polymer brushes, resulted in the formation of tough lubricating films on the rubbing surfaces, thereby tremendously alleviating the friction and wear of tribopair. The impressive anti-rust performance of CDs could be assigned to their strong adsorption ability on metallic surfaces. The above results are hopeful to provide useful information for the design and development of efficient multifunctional additives such as polymer-grafted metallic or inorganic nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polymer brushes
Long-term dispersion stability
Tribological properties
Anti-rust performance
Efficient multifunctional additives

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Dispersion stability of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 in PAO4
Tribological performance of CDs-PDiC12AM-X as a lubricant additive of PAO4
Wear trace of tribopairs and lubrication mechanism analyses
Antirust performance of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 in PAO4

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of (a) DiC12AM and (b) CDs-PDiC12AM.
TEM images with different resolutions and particle size distribution of (a-c) CDs-OH and (d-f) CDs-PDiC12AM-2.
(a) 1H NMR spectra of CDs-OH, and CDs-Br. (b) 1H NMR spectra of CDs-PDiC12AM-2. (c) FTIR spectra of CDs-OH, CDs-Br, and CDs-PDiC12AM-2. (d) TGA curves of various CDs.

(a) CoFs and (b) mean CoFs of the steel/steel tribopair after being treated with the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 dispersions with various concentrations (c, wt%). (c) CoFs of pure PAO4, DiC12AM (1.0 wt%)/, PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/, CDs-PDiC12AM-1 (4.0 wt%)/, CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/, CDs-PDiC12AM-4 (1.0 wt%)/, CDs-PDiC12AM-12 (5.0 wt%)/ and ZDDP (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersions. (d) Mean CoF and wear volume (WV) values of steel substrates treated with the pure PAO4 and CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion under friction for 20 min. (e) CoFs and (f) mean CoFs and WV values of pure PAO4 and CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion across a 120 min friction test. The applied load was 40 N and the sliding frequency was 20 Hz.

Micrograph, 2D and 3D profiles of wear marks on tribopairs lubricated by (a) pure PAO4 and (b) CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion. SEM images of (c) pure PAO4 and (d) CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion abrasion marks after friction and corresponding EDS element mapping results for Fe, Cr, C, O, and N. The load applied in the friction test was 40 N, the frequency of sliding was 20 Hz, the test time was 20 min, and T = 25 °C.

XPS (a) Fe 2p, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s, and (d) N 1s spectra of the wear marks lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion. The load applied in the friction test was 40 N, the frequency of sliding was 20 Hz, the test time was 20 min, and T = 25 °C.
Schematic illustration of the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion.

Photographs of steel rods after immersing in (a) PAO4/distilled water, (b) ZDDP (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion/distilled water, (c) PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion/distilled water, (d) CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion/distilled water, (e) PAO4/seawater, (f) ZDDP (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion/seawater, (g) PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion/seawater, (h) CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion/seawater mixed systems for 4 h at 60 °C. (i) Schematic illustration of the antirust mechanism of the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots grafted by oil-soluble polymer brushes derived from bis-alkyl chain monomers as a lubricant additive of poly-α-olefin with outstanding friction-reducing, anti-wear and anti-rust properties

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of carbon dots (CDs) as lubricant additives of nonpolar oils such as poly-α-olefins (PAOs) has been greatly hindered because most of CDs derived from the bottom-up method were water-soluble. In this work, the oil-soluble polymer brushes grafted CDs were synthesized by the surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization of bis-alkyl chain monomers. As expected, the polymer grafted CDs exhibited the excellent long-term dispersion stability in PAO owing to the principle of similar compatibility. As the lubricant additives of PAO4, the CDs showed the best friction-reducing and anti-wear properties when the polymerization time was 2 h. Adding 1 wt% of CDs could make the coefficient of friction and wear volume of PAO4 reduce by 64.1 % and 49.6 %, respectively. Moreover, both the tribological properties and anti-rust performance of CDs were superior to the commercially used ZDDP. The distinguished friction-reducing and anti-wear properties of CDs were attributed to the synergistic lubrication of carbon cores and polymer brushes. The rolling, repairing and polishing action of carbon cores, together with the tribo-reactions of polymer brushes, resulted in the formation of tough lubricating films on the rubbing surfaces, thereby tremendously alleviating the friction and wear of tribopair. The impressive anti-rust performance of CDs could be assigned to their strong adsorption ability on metallic surfaces. The above results are hopeful to provide useful information for the design and development of efficient multifunctional additives such as polymer-grafted metallic or inorganic nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear not only hinder the relative motion between two contacting surfaces, but also consume nearly 23 % of the world’s total energy consumption [1–3]. A large number of lubricants, including different types of lubricating base oils and additives, have been developed and used to address these critical issues. Poly-α-olefins (PAOs) with good viscosity-temperature properties, low-temperature fluidity, oxidative stability, colorless and non-toxic, are often used to be applied as base stocks for synthetic lubricants in the aerospace and automotive industries [4–6]. However, PAOs as base oils still have some inherent problems. For instance, conventional additives have poor solubility in PAOs and hence often need to be intermixed with esters to better enhance their stability [4,7]. In addition, for economical and practical reasons, lubricant base oils should maintain long-term stability and minimize rusting of metal materials. At the same time, many kinds of lubricant additives, including anticorrosive and antioxidant additives are often added to base oils [8–13]. The complexity and diversity of these components make them be unfavorable for preparation and storage, and even pose health risks to operators [14]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop new methods of green tribology to optimize and improve anti-wear additives and prepare friction modifiers with simple composition, high stability, low friction and rust prevention.

So far, significant advancements have been made in the technology of nanomaterials, which have stood out for various applications in multiple disciplinary fields such as tribology [15]. Investigations have been verified the functions of nanomaterials as additives in lubricants, such as graphene [16,17], graphite nanosheets [18], carbon nanotubes [19], carbon dots [20], graphene quantum dots [21,22], inorganic nanosheets [23–25], and metal nanoparticles [26,27], to provide friction-reducing and anti-wear properties to the lubricants while facilitating heat transfer and reducing the mechanical energy generated in the friction area [28]. Meanwhile, scholars persist in uncovering that the remarkable tribological characteristics of nanoadditives within lubricants are ascribed to the deposition of nanomaterials onto frictional interfaces, facilitating the remediation and refinement of tribopairs [29–31]. Nevertheless, achieving optimal lubrication solely with nanomaterials dispersed in PAO can be not always feasible, prompting the need for organic compounds such as ionic liquids [6,9], poly(ionic liquid)s [32,33], and polyelectrolytes [34,35] are needed to modify the nanomaterials. In contrast to conventional lubricants, which can solely be used to reduce friction in moving parts, the combination of nanomaterials and organic molecular modifiers in PAO is widely considered as one of the most auspicious nanoadditives, given their advantageous tribological properties, rust inhibition, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendliness.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered extensive attention in lubrication research, owing to their environmental sustainability [36], pronounced self-lubricating capabilities [37,38], facile surface modification [39,40], and large surface area [20]. Consequently, they have emerged as the most attractive nanoadditives for PAO lubricants. On the basis of the exceptional tribological properties of CDs, we hope to introduce functions such as rust prevention and corrosion resistance to realize the multi-function of nanoadditives. Polymeric additives have piqued our interest due to their unique advantage of offering design flexibility in both molecular weight and molecular structure [41–43]. Recent studies by Zhao et al. have shown that nanoparticles grafted with poly(lauryl methacrylate) and poly(alkyl methacrylate), respectively, can both be used as lubricant additives, showcasing exceptional dispersibility, stability, and lubricity in nonpolar oils [44,45]. Long-chain molecules with flexible structure enable the polymer to adhere to tribopairs, effectively reducing friction, lowering wear, and regulating the viscosity of the base oil [46–48]. In addition, polymers also have shown admirable corrosion and rust resistance, which can extend the life of equipment by forming a protective film that effectively prevents metal surfaces from oxidizing and rusting [49–52]. Therefore, versatile composite nanoadditives prepared by grafting novel polymers with CDs will show great potential in PAO.

To validate this concept, we used glucose as the carbon source to obtain hydroxyl-rich CDs. Subsequently, a series of oil-soluble polymer-modified CDs-PDiC12AM-X with different polymerization times were synthesized from the initiator-functionalized CDs by surface-initiated atom transfer radical polymerization (SI-ATRP) of bis-alkyl chain monomers [44]. The tribological behavior of CDs-PDiC12AM-X as the additive of PAO4 on the steel/steel tribopair was evaluated by varying the applied load, the sliding frequency, and duration. The rust-proof functions of ZDDP and CDs-PDiC12AM-X were compared when they used as additives for PAO4, respectively. The resultant CDs-PDiC12AM-X/PAO4 oils were found to be stable dispersion, lubricative, and anti-rust. The lubrication mechanism of CDs-PDiC12AM-X was found to be a synergistic effect of the rolling, patching and polishing actions of CDs and the tribochemical reactions of the polymer PDiC12AM. We envision that the results of this research will provide useful information for the design and development of efficient multifunctional additives such as polymer-grafted metallic or inorganic nanomaterials.

Dichloromethane (DCM, 99.5 %), diethyl ether (99.5 %), acetonitrile (99.5 %), anhydrous glucose (99 %), calcium hydride (CaH2, 96 %), triethylamine (TEA, 99 %), anhydrous ethanol (99.7 %), magnesium sulfate anhydrous (MgSO4, 99 %), cuprous chloride (CuCl, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98 %), and glacial acetic acid (98 %) were purchased from the Chengdu Kelong Chemical Regent Co., Ltd. 2-Bromoisobutyryl bromide (BIBB, 98 %), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 99.8 %) were purchased from Beijing Innova Kai Technology Co., Ltd. Acryloyl chloride (97 %) was purchased from Shanghai Bide Pharmatech Co., Ltd. Dodecylamine (98 %), and 1-bromododecane (98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. N,N,N',N'',N′'-Pentamethyldiethyltriamine (PMDETA, 98 %) was provided from Shanghai Saihan Technology Co., Ltd. Zinc dialkyldithiophosphate (ZDDP), and poly-alpha-olefin (PAO4) were obtained from Exxon Mobi Co., Ltd. Before use, TEA, DMF and PMDETA were treated with CaH2 and an activated molecular sieve for water absorption and stored at −4 °C, respectively. Small amounts of oxidized components were removed by soaking CuCl in glacial acetic acid overnight. Then, purified white CuCl was gained by washing with anhydrous ethanol and diethyl ether and drying. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of about 18.25 MΩ cm prepared by the UPH IV ultrapure water purifier (China) was used throughout the experiments. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

Fig. 1a shows the synthesis of the DiC12AM monomer. Specifically, dodecylamine and 1-bromododecane were added to 100 mL of acetonitrile in a 1:1.05 molar ratio and the reaction was refluxed at 80 °C for 6 h. Then, white flaky crystals (DiC12) were obtained after purification by NaOH solution and recrystallization from DCM solution. Subsequently, the acryloyl chloride (1.82 g) solution was slowly added dropwise to DiC12 (0.02 mol) and CH2Cl2 (20 mL) under an ice-water bath. Then, the reaction in the boiling flask-3-neck was continued for 6 h. After the reaction, the lower organic phase was washed to neutral pH and dried with anhydrous MgSO4. Finally, the product of the DiC12AM monomer was gained as a pale-yellow liquid after the partial solvent CH2Cl2 was removed by a rotary evaporator and then transferred to a vacuum oven for low-temperature drying.

Fig. 1b shows the synthesis of CDs-PDiC12AM. According to previous reports, surface initiators modified carbon dots (CDs-Br) were synthesized by us. First, the CDs-OH was gained from glucose were by the ethanol solvothermal method. Specifically, 25 mg of CDs-Br, DiC12AM monomer (0.06 mmol), 5 mL of DMF solution, and PMDETA (0.06 mmol) were added into a 25 mL Schlenk tube respectively. Under a N2 atmosphere, 0.06 mmol of CuCl was introduced into the system, followed immediately by sealing the system with a rubber stopper and quickly transferring it to an oil bath at 50 °C to initiate the polymerization reaction. After the reaction, the crude product was dialyzed in a constantly changed solvent mixture of water/ethanol for 5 days. Finally, the yellowish-brown powder CDs-PDiC12AM was obtained as the final product after treatment with a freeze dryer. By adjusting the polymerization time, CDs-PDiC12AM with different degrees of polymerization can be prepared. The X in CDs-PDiC12AM-X represents the polymerization time, which is 1, 2, 4, and 12 h, respectively.

The most significant lubrication effect of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 was obtained from the tribological performance tests, and thus it was used as an example for all subsequent characterization analyses. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G20) was used to analyze the morphology and size of CDs-OH and CDs-PDiC12AM-2. Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, WQF-520) was employed to analyze the functional groups on CDs- PDiC12AM-2. Thermal gravimetric analyzer (TGA, SDTA85) was used to record the thermal stability of CDs- PDiC12AM-2. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, NEXSA), and proton nuclear magnetic resonance spectrometer (1H NMR, Bruker AVANCE Ⅲ HD 400) were employed to analyze the characterize microstructure of CDs-PDiC12AM-2.

The tribological performances of PAO4 and CDs-PDiC12AM/PAO4 lubricants were examined by a UMT-Trilobal friction and wear tester (UMT, USA) with a reciprocating ball-on disk configuration using the steel ball (AISI 52100, Diameter: 12.7 mm) and disk (AISI 52100, 50 mm × 35 mm × 4 mm, Roughness: 0.014) as counterparts. Both the ball and disk have been cleaned ultrasonically in petroleum ether before the measurements. The sliding frequencies were set to be 5, 10, 20, and 30 Hz, and the applied loads were 40, 60, 80, 100, and 120 N, respectively. The reciprocating distance was 5 mm, and the test durations were 20 and 120 min. All the measurements were conducted three times to ensure credibility.

The wear scars on steel discs were scanned and measured using a three dimensional (3D) light microscope (Bruker ContourGT). The microstructures and elemental compositions of wear scars on steel discs were analyzed by field emission SEM (ZEISS EV0 MA15) with an energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). The tribochemical reactions of CDs-PDiC12AM/PAO4 during the lubrication process were examined by XPS (Thermoscientific NEXSA).

Rust resistance behaviors of the CDs-PDiC12AM/PAO4 (1.0 wt%) and the ZDDP (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 were evaluated according to GB/T 11143-2088 (China). To be more specific, polished steel rods were completely immersed in 300 mL of ZDDP (1.0 wt%)/PAO4, CDs-PDiC12AM (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion and 30 mL of deionized water (or synthetic seawater) under stirring at 60 °C for 4 h. After the steel rods were cleaned with petroleum ether and allowed to dry, the rust grades of the steel rods were determined according to the above standard.

TEM images of CDs-OH ( Fig. 2a and b) reflect that the CDs-OH with spherical morphology and uniform particle size have been successfully synthesized by the solvothermal method of glucose in ethanol. The particle size distribution shows that the average particle size of CDs-OH is 3.07 nm (Fig. 2c). The TEM images of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (Fig. 2d and e) show that it still maintains a spherical morphology, but the average particle size grows to 4.73 nm (Fig. 2f), indicating that the polymer was successfully grafted onto the surface of the carbon dots.

To reveal the synthesis process of CDs-OH, CDs-Br, and CDs-PDiC12AM-2 and their surface information, they were analyzed using 1H NMR spectra, FTIR spectra, XPS spectra, and TGA curves. Fig. 3a displays the 1H NMR spectra of CDs-OH and CDs-Br. The chemical shift peak at δ ∼ 5.32 ppm was ascribed to the proton at the hydroxyl groups in the CDs-OH. The newly appeared peak at δ ∼ 1.89 ppm corresponded to the proton shift of -CH3, meaning that the initiator molecules were successfully grafted on the surfaces of CDs-Br. In the spectrum of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (Fig. 3b), a variety of chemical shift peaks generated by all protons of DiC12AM monomers were fully labeled, indicating that the polymers derived from the bis-alkyl chain monomers were favorably modified to the surfaces of CDs-PDiC12AM-2.

According to FTIR spectra of Fig. 3c, the characteristic absorption peaks at ∼ 3421 cm−1 and ∼1717 cm−1 of CDs-OH can be attributed to the O-H stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups and the -CO stretching vibration of carboxyl groups, respectively, which proves that there are a large number of hydroxyl groups on the surfaces of CDs-OH. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs-Br, the absorption peaks at 1745 cm−1, 1382 cm−1, respectively, and 803 cm−1 can be ascribed to the -CO stretching vibration of ester groups, the C-H stretching and deformation vibration of methyl groups and the C-Br stretching vibration, signifying that the initiator molecules (BIBB) were attached to the surfaces of CDs-Br by ester bonds. In the FTIR spectrum of CDs-PDiC12AM-2, the peaks at 2962 and 2890 cm−1 were derived from the C-H stretching vibrations of methylene and methyl groups, respectively. Meanwhile, the peaks at 1641 and 1363 cm−1 can be due to the tertiary amide stretching vibrations of -CO-N groups and the C-N stretching vibration. Additionally, it is interesting to note that the O-H absorption peak is almost completely disappeared, indicating that the polymer shells were evenly attached to the surfaces of CDs-PDiC12AM-2.

The thermal stability of CDs-OH, CDs-Br, and CDs-PDiC12AM-X (X=1, 2, 4, 12) were analyzed by TGA curves (Fig. 3d). A slight weight loss was generated in the TGA curves below 120 °C, which can be due to the evaporation of moisture. At 200–300 °C, weight loss results from thermal decomposition of organic groups on the surface of the CDs. Subsequently, the mass retention of CDs-OH and CDs-Br were about 55.1 % and 43.5 % at 700 °C, respectively, which means that about 11.6 wt% of the initiator molecules were immobilized on the CDs-Br surfaces. However, the initial weight loss temperature of CDs-PDiC12AM-X is about 350 °C, which is significantly higher than that of CDs-OH and CDs-Br, indicating that the carbon dots after grafting the polymers have outstanding thermal stability. In the full-scan XPS spectra (Figure S1), CDs-OH are mainly composed of C and O elements. A small amount of Br can be detected for CDs-Br. The N elements specific to the polymer can also be found for CDs-PDiC12AM-2. The high-resolution elemental fine spectra shown in Figure S2 minutely describe the C 1 s, O 1 s, Br 3d, and N 1 s elements of various CDs. The above results are consistent with the FTIR analysis, indicating that the CDs-Br and CDs-PDiC12AM-2 were successfully synthesized.

After the synthesis of polymers on the surface of CDs, specific information about the polymers on the surface of CDs-PDiC12AM-2, such as the number average molecular weight (Mn), degree of polymerization (DP), and graft density (GD), can be estimated based on some experimental data and reasonable explanation [32]. The grafting density of the initiator on CDs-Br is shown in Eq.1,
(1)
GD =
N
Br
N
A
S
CD
ρ
CD
V
CD
where, N
Br is the mol concentration of initiator on 1 g of CDs-Br, N
A is Avogadro’s constant, S
CD and V
CD are the surface area and volume of the one-grain CDs, and ρ
CD is the density of CDs. In short, the ρ
CD measured by the pycnometer method is 1.31 g/cm3, and the Br content of CDs-Br is 7.42 wt% by elemental analysis, and hence the N
Br is about 9.29 × 10−4 mol/g. Subsequently, the average particle size of CDs was taken to be 3.07 nm, and the S
CD and V
CD were calculated to be 29.61 nm2 and 15.15 nm3, respectively. Ultimately, the GD was 0.375 initiator/nm2, i.e., the number of initiators on each CDs-Br is about 11.10. Figure S3 shows that the change in conversion of the DiC12AM monomer with polymerization time in the SI-ATRP process follows the first-order kinetics [44]. Finally, the Mn, DP, and GD values of the CDs-PDiC12AM-X were obtained by the previous calculations, and shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion stability of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 in PAO4
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that good compatibility determines whether the lubricant additive will perform optimally in the base oil. In brief, the dispersion stability of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%) in PAO4 was evaluated by simple visual inspection method under ordinary (25 °C) and harsh (-25 °C, and 80 °C) conditions. The CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion under sunlight appears as a yellow-brown transparent solution (Figure S4). Specifically, the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 and PAO4 follow the principle of similar compatibility, endowing CDs-PDiC12AM-2 with preferable dispersion stability in PAO4.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tribological performance of CDs-PDiC12AM-X as a lubricant additive of PAO4
ce:section.ce:para: The tribological behavior of CDs-PDiC12AM-X as the additive of PAO4 on the steel/steel tribopair was evaluated using friction and wear tests. As shown in Fig. S5, a sliding frequency of 20 Hz and an applied load of 40 N were selected as the subsequent friction test conditions by the control variable method. Subsequently, the effect of the concentration of CDs-PDiC12AM-X (X=1, 2, 4, 12) on the tribological properties were investigated. As shown in Figures S6, the coefficient of friction (CoF) curves of CDs-PDiC12AM-X/PAO4 (X=1, 4, 12) dispersions with different concentrations (c, wt%) are all smoother and lower than that of pure base oil (PAO4), which indicates that CDs-PDiC12AM-X (1, 4, 12) have obvious friction-reducing and antiwear action. According to the minimum CoF, the optimal c for CDs-PDiC12AM-1, CDs-PDiC12AM-4, and CDs-PDiC12AM-12 are confirmed as 4.0, 1.0, and 5.0 wt%, respectively. Fig. 4a shows that the steel/steel tribopair after treatment with pure PAO4 presents high CoF values at 0.24 under the applied load of 40 N and sliding frequency of 20 Hz. By contrast, CoF of various CDs-PDiC12AM-2/PAO4 dispersions with various c all exhibited intuitive decrease and smoothness. As shown in Fig. 4b, the CDs-PDiC12AM-2/PAO4 dispersion reached an optimum value at a CDs-PDiC12AM-2 concentration of 1.0 wt% with a mean CoF lower than 0.08. Meanwhile, the mean CoFs of the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 are all relatively stable and does not change significantly with increasing c.

The specific effects of optimum c on the tribological performance of CDs-PDiC12AM-X, DiC12AM-2 and PDiC12AM-2 were compared. Fig. 4c shows that the friction-reducing of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 is much better than that of CDs-PDiC12AM-1 and CDs-PDiC12AM-12, but not much different from that of CDs-PDiC12AM-4. The monomer DiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%) molecules as additives show a slight CoF reduction for the base oil (PAO4), indicating ordinary tribological properties. Meanwhile, it can be seen that CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%) and CDs-PDiC12AM-12 (5.0 wt%) additives have lower CoF than the commercially used ZDDP. Specifically, the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 at c of 1.0 wt% as an additive can reduce the CoF and WV of PAO4 by 64.1 % and 49.6 %, respectively (Fig. 4d). The polymer of PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%) was synthesized by the SI-ATRP method due to the fact that the molecular weight of a polymer grafted onto nanoparticles was similar to that of the free polymer derived from the free initiator. One can see that the presence of 1.0 wt% PDiC12AM-2 nearly halved the CoF of a PAO4-based dispersion. The free polymer of PDiC12AM-2 exhibits improved friction reduction properties compared with the DiC12AM-2 molecule. The above results indicate that the carbon cores and the polymer brushes on their surfaces play a synergistic lubrication effect. For liquid lubricating materials, the ruggedness of lubricant additives is an essential characteristic that cannot be ignored. After the friction time was increased to 120 min, the CoF curve of PAO4 was higher in the early stage, fluctuating above and below 0.2 (Fig. 4e), which proved the poor lubricity of the base oil. In contrast, the CoF curve of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion was lower and stable over 120 min, approximating as low as 0.05. As shown in Fig. 5f, the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion can reduce the CoF and WV of PAO4 by 68.8 % and 60 %, respectively, during 120 min of friction. In other words, the lubrication properties of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 hardly decay with increasing friction time, reflecting the robustness of its tribological properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wear trace of tribopairs and lubrication mechanism analyses
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the antiwear properties and lubrication mechanism of CDs-PDiC12AM-X as additives, the morphologies, elemental compositions, and chemical states of wear marks on the surface of the steel disks were characterized in detail by SEM, EDS, XPS analyses and the use of a 3D light microscope. When pure PAO4 is used as a lubricant, there is wear tracks with the widths of 0.5272 mm and 0.7653 mm on the steel disk, respectively, during 20 min (Fig. 5a) and 120 min of friction (Figure S7a). More severe abrasion can be found in the refined 2D and 3D profiles, and the depth of the wear tracks are ∼15.41 μm and ∼18.23 μm, respectively. However, when the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 is introduced into the base oil of PAO4, there is wear tracks with the widths of 0.4913 mm and 0.8661 mm on the steel disk, respectively, during 20 min (Fig. 5b) and 120 min of friction (Figure S7b). The depths of the corresponding wear tracks become shallower, to 11.92 μm and 5.51 μm, respectively. Meanwhile, when the commercially used ZDDP is introduced to the base oil of PAO4, there is wear tracks with a width of 0.5112 mm and depth of 12.14 μm on the steel disk at 20 min of friction (Figure S8). In contrast, the friction properties of ZDDP (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion are slightly worse than those of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion. The CDs-PDiC12AM-X/PAO4 dispersions can significantly reduce the wear of the tribopairs due to the possibility of adsorbing to the steel disk to reduce the direct contact between the steel ball and the disk.

The morphologies and element distributions of the wear tracks were observed by SEM images and detected by EDS analyses (Fig. 5). Deep grooves, holes, cracks and wear debris can be clearly seen on wear track lubricated by pure PAO4. However, the surface of the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion lubricated steel disk is so smooth with almost no wear, crack, or pit. The above results demonstrate that the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion can significantly reduce the wear of the tribopair compared with pure PAO4. According to the EDS mapping results of the tribopair surface, the selected area was abundant in Fe, C, and O elements. The reason for the higher C element in this case may be the thermal decomposition of PAO4 during friction, leading to lubrication failure [41]. In contrast, the presence of a small amount of N elements along with the uniform distribution of Fe, Cr, C, and O elements on the surface of the abrasion marks confirms the involvement of the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 as additives in the generation of boundary lubrication films, which effectively prevents the direct contact of the rough peaks of the tribopair.

XPS characterizations on the steel disks were employed to demonstrate the friction-reducing and antiwear mechanism of the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion. The high-resolution elemental XPS spectra show the chemical states of C, O, Fe, and N elements on the wear marks lubricated by PAO4 and CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion ( Fig. 6). The Fe 2p spectrum shows that the steel substrate after lubricated with the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion displayed Fe-C peaks at around 708.8 eV that can be attributed to the friction chemical reaction of the polymer to produce ferric carbides. As the same time, our findings can be verified by the observation of the Fe-C peak at 284.5 eV in the C 1 s spectrum [53]. Comparison of the high-resolution C 1 s and N 1 s spectrum reveals that not only the C-N peak at 285.6 eV but also the NH peak (399.7 eV) of the amide group appear when the wear track was lubricated by the CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion. The oxidation and thermal decomposition of PAO4 resulted in a high C-O content and low Fe-O content on the wear mark, corresponding to the results of EDS analyses. In addition, the high C-C/CC/C-N content can be attributed to the deposition of carbon cores of CDs-PDiC12AM-2, which help to effectively reduce the mechanical energy generated during the frictional process. The XPS analysis results demonstrate that the polymer brushes on the surface of the carbon dots undergo a complex friction chemical reaction with the tribopair during the frictional process, which results in the generation of a tough lubricating film.

Based on the experimental results above, we propose the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 as an additive of PAO4. On the one hand, in CDs-PDiC12AM-2/PAO4 dispersion, the rolling, repairing and polishing effects of CDs can significantly alleviate the degradation of base oil PAO4 due to friction. The polymer molecular brushes on CDs-PDiC12AM-2 not only promote their own dispersion stability in the base oil PAO4, but also enhance the thickness of a lubricant film between the friction surfaces, thus improving anti-wear and friction-reducing performance. On the other hand, the polymer on CDs-PDiC12AM-2 contains active N elements that can undergo complex friction chemical reactions with the steel substrate during friction, resulting in the formation of a boundary lubrication film consisting of the friction products Fe3C and carbon cores of CDs-PDiC12AM-2. We schematically elaborate the lubrication mechanism of CDs-PDiC12AM-2/PAO4 dispersion in Fig. 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antirust performance of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 in PAO4
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 8a and e, large rust spots appeared on the steel rods whether they were immersed in PAO4/distilled water or in PAO4/seawater for 4 h at 60 °C, and the rusted area obviously exceeded 5 % of the surface area of the rods. It can be seen that the antirust property of base oil PAO4 needs to be further strengthened, especially under long time use and harsh conditions. ZDDP, a commonly used lubricant additive with antioxidant and anticorrosion properties, was selected as a control group [54]. As shown in Fig. 8b and f, 1.0 wt% of ZDDP was added to PAO4/distilled water and PAO4/seawater systems for the antirust test, and a small amount of rust spots appeared on both surfaces of the steel rods. However, the addition of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%) to the PAO4/distilled water and PAO4/seawater systems was tested for antirust, and interestingly the surface of the steel rods showed virtually no rust spots in both environments (Fig. 8d and h). Meanwhile, the addition of PDi12AM-2(1.0 wt%) to the PAO4/distilled water and PAO4/seawater systems was tested for antirust, and in both environments (Fig. 8c and g), only a small amount of rust spots appeared on the surface of the steel rods, which was only second to the rust prevention ability of the CDs-PDi12AM-2(1.0 wt%) additive. For the comparison of the above experimental results, we found that CDs-PDiC12AM-2 had excellent antirust properties as a lubricating additive for PAO4. As shown in Fig. 8i, CDs-PDiC12AM-2 features polar groups and long alkyl chains, enabling strong adsorption onto the metal surface. The oil-soluble double alkyl chains form a stable protective film, oriented perpendicular to the steel rods, effectively preventing rust and oxidation from corrosive agents like oxygen, water, and other corrosive mediums. The dense hydrophobic antirust film formed in this way provides good antirust protection and extends the service life of metal materials in special environments [55].

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized the multifunctional additive CDs-PDiC12AM-X for PAO4 by the SI-ATRP method. The polymer molecular brushes on the surface of CDs-PDiC12AM-X not only give it outstanding dispersion stability in PAO4, but also enhance the thickness and toughness of the lubricant film between the friction surfaces, which facilitated polymer adhesion on the surface of steel disc. CDs-PDiC12AM-X as an additive in base oil PAO4 showed excellent lubrication and antirust properties, with CDs-PDiC12AM-2 having the optimal lubrication performance. CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion could reduce the CoF and WV of base oil by 64.1 % and 49.6 %, respectively, which resulted from the synergistic lubrication of the carbon cores and polymers of CDs-PDiC12AM. The rolling, repairing and polishing action of the carbon cores, together with the polymer molecular brushes, formed a tough lubricating film containing the friction product Fe3C, which insulated the tribopair from direct contact, thus greatly reducing friction and wear. The adsorption of CDs-PDiC12AM on metallic materials gave them high antirust performance. The long-term dispersion stability, lubricity, and antirust properties of the CDs-PDiC12AM as an additive have been demonstrated. Our work may provide new ideas for the exploration of new materials and for the study of interface science.

Hongsheng Lu: Validation, Supervision, Software, Project administration, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Baogang Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Qingqing Chen: Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Siwei Chen: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.
The Supporting Information is available free of charge.

XPS full-scan and fine spectra of various CDs; conversion rate of DiC12AM monomer with polymerization time; rough Mn, Dp, and GD values of CDs-PDiC12AM-X, images of CDs-PDiC12AM-2 (1.0 wt%)/PAO4 dispersion at −15 °C, 25 °C, 80 °C for 3 months; CoF curves and mean CoF of CDs-PDiC12AM-2/PAO4 at 40 N and different frequencies; mean CoF and mean WV of CDs-PDiC12AM-2/PAO4 at 20 Hz and different loads; CoF curves and mean CoF of CDs-PDiC12AM-X/PAO4 lubricants with various concentrations (c, wt%) under a load of 40 N, a frequency of 20 Hz and a duration of 20 min; micrograph, 2D and 3D profiles of wear marks on tribopairs lubricated by different lubricants under a load of 40 N, a frequency of 20 Hz and a duration of 120 min.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51975493).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2024.135392.
